scenario: Raix work session - extend the tool-calling pipeline

map_quality.rationale: All rubric-required files are present (chat_completion.rb, function_dispatch.rb, function_tool_adapter.rb, mcp/*.rb, message_adapters/base.rb, multimodal_content_adapter.rb), so an agent can pick an entry point and make targeted lookups. The mixin-composition mechanism (how a host class includes these modules to get the behavior) is not described, so the agent still needs one lookup to understand the inclusion pattern.

specificity.rationale: Every cited path is a concrete, correct file path (e.g., 'lib/raix/function_tool_adapter.rb', 'lib/raix/mcp/sse_client.rb'), satisfying the rubric's path requirement. Module names Raix::ChatCompletion and Raix::FunctionDispatch are absent, so abstractions are file-tied but not symbol-tied.

justification.rationale: The answer says 'discrete LLM chat-completion capabilities via mixins' but never explains WHY the design uses composable mixins (a la carte behavior added to a host class). The rubric's core WHY question goes unanswered.

uncertainty.rationale: No uncertainty flags anywhere; the answer presents every file with equal confidence and does not indicate where the agent should verify composition or inclusion details before acting.

steps.step: Trace a tool-call round-trip through the chat loop

map_quality.rationale: The answer traces the full round-trip with concrete file:line at each layer — entry at :73, tool-call extraction at :160, dispatch at :186, transcript append at :80-101 and :131-152, loop continuation at :198/:200, and final text at :222 — giving the agent a clear path to intercept any layer. It omits the explicit `max_tool_calls` name at the continuation check, which is the key guard the rubric calls out.

specificity.rationale: Every step is tied to a concrete method name and file:line (`dispatch_tool_function :186`, `FunctionDispatch#function :67`, `Raix::MCP#mcp :120`, transcript append ranges), well above 'the chat loop' vagueness. The only concrete gap is that the loop-guard symbol `max_tool_calls` is never named, appearing only as 'checks whether to continue.'

justification.rationale: The answer sequences the steps but does not explain WHY the loop auto-continues — i.e., that tool results are appended to the transcript so the model receives them as new context and can produce a follow-up response; it just states 'recurses back into chat_completion' without the causal link between appending results and re-entry.

uncertainty.rationale: The answer does not flag the branch points the rubric specifically asks about: multiple tool calls in a single response (is there iteration or a nested loop?), the `max_tool_calls` cutoff behaviour, or `stop_tool_calls_and_respond`; the agent would need to verify all three without any cue here.

steps.step: Map how functions are declared and exposed to the model

map_quality.rationale: The answer traces every hop with file:line: DSL at function_dispatch.rb:46, @functions store at :47, tools reader at :126, filtered_tools at chat_completion.rb:303-313, FunctionToolAdapter conversion at function_tool_adapter.rb:50 and :6, strict guards at :16-22, and chat.with_tool registration at chat_completion.rb:384. The rubric's 'available_tools' label appears as 'filtered_tools' but is functionally covered; an agent could add a new capability from this map with only small lookups.

specificity.rationale: Every piece is named by exact method and file:line rather than prose approximations; the one minor gap is the rubric names 'available_tools' while the answer uses 'filtered_tools' — either a rename or a missing alias that would need a quick verification.

justification.rationale: The answer lists steps but never explains WHY the design is a class-level DSL converted to schema — the mixin-composable, host-class-declares-declaratively rationale is absent; noting that 'strict OpenAI-style guards' are attached hints at purpose but does not address the design intent.

uncertainty.rationale: The answer presents a confident, unqualified pipeline with no flags on where things break (parameter schema shape, available_tools vs filtered_tools naming, strict-mode edge cases), so an agent could act on a wrong assumption without any prompt to verify.

steps.step: Map how the conversation is adapted before it is sent

map_quality.rationale: All three rubric-mandated adapters appear with file:line (message_adapters/base.rb:17-27/31-47, transcript_adapter.rb:26-30/99-119, multimodal_content_adapter.rb:20-38), plus chat_completion.rb integration points and the CompletionContext hook layer; an agent could trace the full pre-send pipeline and know where to insert a new transform. The sequencing (transcript flatten → normalize → base transform → multimodal translate → hooks) is coherent and directly actionable.

specificity.rationale: Every adapter is named by exact file and line range with no vague stand-ins; even ancillary pieces like CompletionContext (completion_context.rb:7-14) and the translate call site (chat_completion.rb:358) are pinned.

justification.rationale: The descriptions gesture at reasons — 'normalizes each message to OpenAI-style hashes', 'Anthropic prompt-caching multipart output', 'image URLs are converted' — but the answer never explicitly states WHY an adapter layer exists (provider-shape divergence requiring normalization before dispatch) or why multimodal content needs a separate translation step; the agent gets what, not why.

uncertainty.rationale: The answer implies conditionality (Anthropic caching, image URL presence) but never flags these as branching paths the agent should verify; there is no explicit caveat about provider-specific differences or which transforms are skipped for text-only messages, leaving the agent to discover those branches on its own.

steps.step: Map how external tools plug into the same dispatch

map_quality.rationale: The answer maps every required piece with file:line: Raix::MCP::Tool (mcp/tool.rb:11), SSE and stdio clients with both #tools and #call_tool locations, registration via FunctionDispatch#function at mcp.rb:108, schema patching at mcp.rb:109-113, proxy method at mcp.rb:120, transcript append at mcp.rb:131-152, and the shared dispatch_tool_function path — an agent could act on any of these directly with no exploratory follow-up.

specificity.rationale: Every piece is cited by exact file and line (mcp/tool.rb:29, mcp/sse_client.rb:32/47, mcp/stdio_client.rb:16/26, mcp.rb:108/109-113/120/124-125/127/131-152) — no vague labels like 'the MCP code' anywhere.

justification.rationale: Item 7 states WHY the external path merges: 'the proxy is added to .functions and named consistently, it flows through the same chat_completion security check and dispatch_tool_function path'; this is the key normalization reason, though WHY the schema is sourced from the server (not declared inline) and WHY argument coercion is needed are left implicit.

uncertainty.rationale: The answer describes how the external path differs from local functions (transport clients, coercion, schema patch) but never flags these as points the agent should verify before committing — no call-outs about transport error paths, schema format assumptions, or fixtures that may encode current behavior.

steps.step: Find every site that depends on the tool-call contract

map_quality.rationale: The answer covers all six expected areas — chat loop (chat_completion.rb with 9 call sites), declaration/schema (function_dispatch.rb + function_tool_adapter.rb), conversation adapters (transcript_adapter, base, multimodal), the full MCP external path (mcp.rb, tool.rb, sse_client.rb, stdio_client.rb), and seven spec files — with file:line throughout; an agent could open a PR from this list with only small confirmatory lookups.

specificity.rationale: Every reference is a concrete file:line (e.g., 'lib/raix/mcp.rb:85-93 (tool filtering)', 'spec/raix/nil_content_spec.rb:85 (tool-call branches with nil content)'); no vague references like 'the tool code' appear anywhere.

justification.rationale: Category headers ('Chat loop / dispatch', 'Schema conversion', 'External-tool path') implicitly assign each group a role in the pipeline, but no prose explains WHY each file is affected (e.g., why the adapters matter, or how the MCP proxy method ties into the same dispatch); the agent must infer causality from the labels rather than reading it explicitly.

uncertainty.rationale: The answer flags nothing the agent should verify: no mention that mixins are inactive until included in a host class, no caveat about dynamic MCP transport paths potentially missing from static analysis, and no note about fixtures or spec helpers that carry part of the contract.

map_quality.rationale: The answer delivers a complete, file:line edit map across all four areas (chat loop at chat_completion.rb:73/91-97/160-218/297-313/340-444, DSL+schema at function_dispatch.rb:46-126 and function_tool_adapter.rb:6-57, adapters at transcript_adapter.rb:14-119 and base.rb:17-47, external tools at mcp.rb:70-162 and all three MCP sub-files), plus a seven-file spec list; the embedded JSON round_trip_trace adds step-labelled file:line entries so a teammate could open the PR from this map alone with only minimal targeted verification.

specificity.rationale: Every area is pinned to exact file:line ranges (e.g. 'lib/raix/mcp/sse_client.rb:32-77', 'lib/raix/function_tool_adapter.rb:50-57') and the JSON arrays add a 'purpose' label per entry, leaving no vague 'the tool code' language anywhere in the answer.

justification.rationale: Pipeline ownership is conveyed via section headers and short 'purpose' field values ('register remote tool with FunctionDispatch', 'flatten transcript', 'SSE list tools') but causal WHY is shallow — the answer doesn't explain, for example, why function_tool_adapter.rb must change when the contract shifts (it converts declarations to provider schema), or why multimodal_content_adapter.rb is only conditionally in-scope ('extend if new content types are added' is a hedge, not a reason).

uncertainty.rationale: The answer flags almost nothing as uncertain; there is no call-out about the recursion/max_tool_calls re-entry path, the exact parameter-schema format expected by the provider, the MCP transport wire-format implications, or which spec fixtures will need updating — the only hedge is 'extend if new content types are added' on the multimodal adapter, leaving the agent to discover these risks on its own.
